title: Gift Card Purchase with Recipient Email Mismatch and Value Laddering
narrative: Fraudster purchases gift cards (ProductCD=W) using stolen card credentials with purchaser email (P_emaildomain) set to a disposable domain like tempmail.com or guerrillamail.com, while recipient email (R_emaildomain) routes to a different, longer-established domain. The attacker tests card validity with a $15-25 initial purchase, then rapidly escalates to $200-500 transactions within 15-30 minutes if successful. This laddering behavior exploits the gift-card merchant category's delayed fraud detection while the mismatched email pairing obscures the beneficial owner. Typically occurs during high-volume shopping windows (10am-2pm) when manual review queues are saturated. The P_emaildomain often shows card1_txn_count_1day=1, indicating first use, while TransactionAmt progression creates a distinctive step pattern across sequential transactions to the same MID.
indicators: P_emaildomain belongs to disposable email service list, R_emaildomain is established provider (gmail, outlook, yahoo)
ProductCD=W (gift card/digital goods) with 3+ transactions in 20-minute window showing value laddering: $15-25, then $75-150, then $200-500
card1_txn_count_1day=1 or 2, indicating new card usage to this merchant
TransactionAmt increases 5x-20x between first and third transaction to same merchant
Transactions cluster in 10am-2pm window (txn_hour=10-14) during weekdays
dist1 (distance between billing and shipping) null or >500km when addresses provided
R_emaildomain shows card1_email_count_7day â‰¥3, suggesting recipient email reused across multiple cards
summary: A disposable email purchases gift cards with rapidly escalating amounts—starting at $15-25, then jumping to $200-500 within minutes—while the recipient email is a different, established domain. The purchaser's card shows first-time use, and transactions cluster during busy daytime hours.
reasoning: The fraudster tests stolen card credentials with a small gift card purchase, then quickly maxes out the card before detection, sending value to a separate email they control. Mismatched sender/recipient emails and value laddering indicate deliberate obfuscation rather than a legitimate customer gradually increasing their purchase.
indicator_explanations: Buyer uses throwaway email while recipient gets legitimate long-term provider
Fraudster tests card limits with small gift cards before maxing out
Card appears brand new to this merchant with minimal transaction history
Purchase amounts escalate dramatically from initial test to final drain
Activity concentrated in midday weekday hours suggesting automated attack pattern
Billing and shipping locations are impossibly far apart or suspiciously missing
Same recipient email collects gift cards from multiple different payment cards
